comments.text: Apparently before the St. Lawrence Seaway opened when they wanted to get large ships into the Great Lakes they just brought them up the Mississippi and shoved them through the Chicago River

(in particular the opening of the St. Lawrence Seaway allowed ocean-going ships to navigate into the Great Lakes and made a bunch of midwest barge canals functionally pointless. That happened in the mid 20th century, not the 1840s)

strange but true: while new york struggled to build the erie canal to bring western grains and produce to market, farmers flocked to chicago to put their wares on boats, where the captains would then head out into lake michigan to sail around in circles yelling "weeeeeeeee!"

Erie Canal was built to access western NY and the Great Lakes, it was completed in 1825 Chicago was founded in 1833, in fact it was the Canal that made Chicago viable as a mass settlement and 2 streets in Chicago are named for the canal and its main proponent. I assume ChatGPT wrote this mess

The St. Lawrence Seaway was one of the most important construction projects in Canadian history.
It allows ships to reach the Great Lakes from the Atlantic Ocean, but the project came at a cost to 6,500 Canadians.
This is the story of The Lost Villages. 🧵 1/17
Glad to hear Lake Ontario has finally gotten a direct connection to the Atlantic without that pesky St. Lawrence Seaway. Shame about my home state of NJ sinking into the ocean though.

Yes. Illinois surveyed the site in 1830 and Chicago was chartered in 1837. It's also historically illiterate in a different way. The Erie Canal linked the Great Lakes to the Atlantic, with New York and (eventually) Chicago as entrepots at opposite ends of the same network of commerce.

So like, Canadians, how much are we thinking per ship trying to transit out of Seattle to the Pacific or Great Lakes to the Atlantic? Not too much to start until we get AShM batteries and OWA drones online.

one could make the argument the Erie Canal helped make "Chicago" and much of the midwest lake development practicable. it allowed faster/cheaper trade between the atlantic ocean and the great lakes. but it wasn't built to compete with Chicago
Came here to say this! The Erie Canal provided access to the Great Lakes from the Atlantic and that’s what spurred the insane growth of chicago and led it to become the nation’s hub of commerce.

Nov. 30, 1829:
The Welland Canal officially opened as two ships passed from Port Dalhousie to Port Robinson, Upper Canada. The Canal linked Lake Erie and Lake Ontario. The canal would be expanded several times. Learn more 👇
For an actual history of the Erie Canal, check out "The Death and Life of the Great Lakes" by Dan Egan!

The #Ohio and #ErieCanal was constructed in the 1820s and 1830s and essentially connected #LakeErie with the #OhioRiver and also had connections to other canal systems in #Pennsylvania. This small remaining portion is near #CirclevilleOhio. #photography
Great Lakes shipping strike ends. UAW reaches tentative deal with Stellantis after dealing with Ford last week. No agreement with General Motors as that strike expands.

the genesee valley canal has the illinois & michigan beat by 8 years, connecting the allegheny river to the erie canal (and by extension, the great lakes and st. lawrence). so the i&m canal only turned the eastern midwest into an island

The Portage Canal, built to connect the Fox and Wisconsin rivers and thereby the Great Lakes and Mississippi basins, but quickly made obsolete by railroads. A remnant of the early 19th century canal-building era, as seen in in Portage and out in the country to the northeast

200 years ago today, October 26, 1825: The Erie Canal was ceremonially opened in Buffalo, New York. The 363-mile-long artificial waterway stretched from Albany on the Hudson River to Buffalo on Lake Erie, connecting the Great Lakes to the Atlantic Ocean. It transformed Buffalo into a major hub.

The Erie Canal, opened 200 years ago, transformed trade and the United States – and also ecology. It cut freight costs by 90% but also introduced invasive species like sea lampreys to the Great Lakes, devastating fisheries. buff.ly/CmPmB2d

The Erie Canal was built 1817-21, Chicago was incorporated in 1837. The only thing the two have in common is that the canal *accelerated* settlement of the Great Lakes region, and was definitely not built to "compete with Chicago." Cuomo is either stupid or lying, which could it be?

#OnThisDay in 1825, the #ErieCanal opened to shipping traffic. The new waterway linked the Hudson River and the Great Lakes, accelerating westward expansion & the growth of #CivilEngineering knowledge in the US. More info: www.eriecanal.org/index.html #ErieCanal200 #histSTM #infrastructure 🗃️📜

Leadership from AGLPA and Great Lakes St. Lawrence Seaway Development Corp. visited to discuss ways we can increase interlake trade across Lake Erie and throughout our Great Lakes region. It is vital to build upon existing trade relationships with Canada, our largest international trading partner.

The Erie Canal, completed 200 years ago, transformed America's economy, explains a historian. Although the canal idea was widely criticized as impractical and against “God’s will”, within a few years, shipping rates from Lake Erie to New York City fell by over 90%.

A lot of this is geographic. The United States' unusually well distributed economic growth in the 19th and 20th century is mostly thanks to us lucking out into a top tier river network that gave incredibly wide access to oceanic trade. You could ship steel from the Great Lakes to New York by boat.

On this day, October 26, 1925, 200 years ago, the Erie Canal opened to traffic. The canal linked the Great Lakes Region with the Atlantic Ocean and served as an example of how the west of North America might link with its East Coast.

The Illinois & Michigan Canal, opened in 1848, is why Chicago is. Why did it work when most failed? It actually solved a problem! It connected the Great Lakes with the Mississippi River, creating a shipping route from the Atlantic Ocean down through the U.S. It’s been replaced by bigger canals.

200 years ago today, November 4, 1825: Governor DeWitt Clinton's flotilla completed a 10-day voyage from Buffalo to New York City in celebration of the opening of the Erie Canal. Clinton performed a symbolic "Wedding of the Waters" by pouring a keg of Lake Erie water into the Atlantic Ocean.

NYC is also just geographically advantageous for business. It is a great port city, connected to the Great lakes and a ton of river systems for shipping things throughout the country via barge. Which is why it is where it is. And we spent centuries building infrastructure specifically around it.
